local_names.romanized_local: Gālí yútóu (Mandarin); Kari Kepala Ikan (Malay); Meen thalai kari (Tamil)

food_subgroup: Fish head in spiced coconut milk curry with vegetables

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Singapore Fish Head Curry. Nearest parent is a fish curry or coconut-based fish dish. A new FoodOn concept is recommended for this Singaporean signature dish.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish curry — spiced fish in coconut milk (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Singapore Fish Head Curry. A02VG (fish curry, coconut milk-based) is the closest defensible parent code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Singapore Fish Head Curry as a specific food entity.

serving.description: 1 serving (fish head + curry + vegetables, no rice)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Singapore Fish Head Curry is approximately 400 g, comprising a portion of fish head meat, curry gravy, and accompanying vegetables such as okra, brinjal, and tomatoes, without rice.

nutrient_source.title: Fish Head Curry — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F03020006
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo Database under Food Code F03020006. This is an exact match for Singapore Fish Head Curry. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central analogous fish curry entries where HPB data was not available, as documented in per_100g_meta.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Dishes and Curry-Based Foods — Analogous Estimate from University of Sydney GI Database; fish-based curry closest analogous entry
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore Fish Head Curry in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on: fish protein (GI ~0), coconut milk fat (GI ~0), okra (GI 20–35, University of Sydney GI Database analogous vegetables), eggplant (GI 15–30), tomatoes (GI ~15), and tamarind (low GI acidulant). The dish is dominated by fish protein and fat from coconut milk, with only ~4.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, yielding a low GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 18.0) / 100 = 7.2, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 18.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — approximately 518 mg per 100 g, equating to ~2072 mg sodium per standard 400 g serving, which represents approximately 90% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200 g per meal and avoid adding additional salt or fish sauce, as a single full serving provides close to the entire daily sodium allowance.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~4.5 g per 100 g) with a low glycemic index of approximately 40; high-quality fish protein supports satiety without significant glycaemic impact.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Singapore Fish Head Curry is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without rice or with a small portion of low-GI rice, as the curry itself contributes minimal carbohydrate and has a low glycemic load of approximately 7.2 per 400 g serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (3.2 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium (518 mg per 100 g) are the primary cardiovascular concerns, partially offset by omega-3 fatty acids from red snapper or grouper and anti-inflammatory compounds from turmeric and curry leaves.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume this dish in moderate portions (200–300 g), benefiting from the omega-3 content of the fish head, but should be mindful of the saturated fat from coconut milk and the high sodium load, and should pair it with potassium-rich foods to help offset sodium effects.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from red snapper and grouper; anti-inflammatory curcumin from turmeric; antioxidants from tamarind and curry leaves.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Singapore Fish Head Curry may be beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the anti-inflammatory properties of fish omega-3 fatty acids and turmeric curcumin, though the high sodium content warrants moderation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Singapore Fish Head Curry (咖喱鱼头 / Kari Kepala Ikan / மீன் தலை கறி) is one of Singapore's most iconic dishes and a celebrated example of Singaporean Indian-Chinese culinary fusion. The dish features a large, halved head of red snapper (ikan merah) or grouper (kerapu / garoupa), simmered in a rich, tangy, mildly spicy curry gravy made from a South Indian-style spice base of turmeric, coriander, cumin, and chilli, enriched with coconut milk and balanced with tamarind paste for characteristic sourness. Accompanying vegetables — okra (ladies' fingers / bendi), eggplant (brinjal), tomatoes, and deep-fried tofu puffs (tau pok) — are cooked in the same gravy, absorbing its complex flavours. The fish head yields gelatinous, richly flavoured meat from the cheeks, collar, and eye socket, which are prized for their texture. Nutritionally, a 400 g serving provides approximately 452 kcal, 50 g protein, 22 g fat (12.8 g saturated), and 18 g carbohydrate, with notable selenium (88 mcg), vitamin B12 (11.2 mcg), niacin (19.2 mg), and vitamin D (22 mcg). Sodium is high at ~2072 mg per serving. The dish has a low glycemic index (~40) and a glycemic load of ~7.2 per serving, making it suitable for diabetics when consumed without rice. It is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Credited to a Tamil Indian cook in 1940s Singapore who adapted a South Indian fish curry for Chinese customers, it is traditionally shared among family or friends and served with plain rice or bread.

can a diabetic eat fish head curry
fish head curry protein per serving
Singapore fish head curry vs Malay fish curry

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and most micronutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo Database (Food Code F03020006), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Three minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central analogous entries with confidence 0.9. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology. Overall confidence is high given the exact HPB source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Singapore Fish Head Curry not available in HPB Foodo Database (F03020006) or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB Foodo Database for this entry. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source). Copper (0.06 mg/100g), manganese (0.15 mg/100g), and vitamin K (12.0 mcg/100g) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central analogous fish curry entries (confidence 0.9) as these micronutrients are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo record. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Singapore Fish Head Curry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (40 × 18.0) / 100 = 7.2 g. Hypertension caution: a single 400 g serving provides ~2072 mg sodium (~90% of the 2300 mg WHO daily limit). The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free under standard preparation; allergen note — tofu puffs (tau pok) contain soy.
